highlights.content: # Nvidia doesn’t stop: Invests $2 billion in AI hardware giant ## Nvidia announces strategic partnership with Marvell, including a $2 billion investment, aiming to develop advanced AI infrastructure and support innovative technologies. Nvidia. (photo credit: REUTERS) By[MAARIV ONLINE](/author/maariv-online) According to an official report, NVIDIA and Marvell have signed a strategic partnership connecting Marvell to NVIDIA’s AI system, focusing on advanced AI infrastructure and intelligent networks. The partnership is intended to give customers greater flexibility in developing next-generation computing infrastructure, using advanced connectivity and optical technologies. As part of the agreement, NVIDIA invested $2 billion in Marvell. The company will provide custom chips and communication networks, while NVIDIA will provide supporting technologies, including compute, storage, and networking components. The platform enables seamless integration of all components, allowing AI infrastructure to operate more efficiently and as a unified system. The two companies are also collaborating to transform global communication networks into AI infrastructure and advance next-generation networking solutions, including silicon photonics and optical interconnects. Jensen Huang, CEO and founder of NVIDIA, said: “Demand for token generation is rising, and the world is rushing to build AI factories. Together with Marvell, we enable customers to leverage NVIDIA’s AI ecosystem and build advanced, customized AI computing.” Matt Murphy, Chairman and CEO of Marvell, said: “The expanded partnership with NVIDIA highlights the importance of high-speed connectivity, optical interconnects, and large-scale accelerated AI infrastructure. The combination of Marvell’s expertise in custom silicon with NVIDIA’s AI ecosystem will enable customers to build efficient and scalable AI infrastructures.” The report notes that the partnership will focus on providing solutions for enterprise customers and building large-scale customized AI computing infrastructure, while maintaining full compatibility with NVIDIA systems and expanding opportunities for innovation and development. ### See more on [technology](/tags/technology)| [investment](/tags/investment)| [innovation](/tags/innovation)| [infrastructure](/tags/infrastructure)| [Nvidia](/tags/nvidia)| [AI](/tags/ai)| [company](/tags/company)| [partnership](/tags/partnership) ---

highlights.content: 1. [Home](/) 2. [Hans](/hans) 3. [Education & Careers](/hans/education-careers) [Hans](/hans) # Hybrid Workforce: How human agents and AI agents are working together * Created On:  5 April 2026 7:34 PM IST [By The Hans India](/) ##### Share : X Not long ago, “help” at work meant asking a teammate, searching an internal doc, or raising a ticket and waiting. Today, many teams are adding a new kind of colleague into the mix: AI agents that can draft, summarize, search, route, and sometimes even execute tasks across tools. The result is a hybrid workforce where humans and AI agents share the load, each doing what they are best at. This shift is not small. Microsoft’s 2024 Work Trend Index reported that 75% of knowledge workers use AI at work. That tells you something important: AI is no longer “coming.” It is already embedded in day-to-day work, often driven by employees who want speed and relief from repetitive tasks. **Copilots are becoming coworkers** For a while, workplace AI was mostly assistive. It suggested a reply, helped write a paragraph, or summarized meeting notes. Now, AI is moving toward agentic behavior, meaning it does not just suggest, it can take steps. It can look up information, fill forms, update systems, generate reports, and follow a workflow across multiple tools. That is why the term ‘agent’ matters. An AI agent is not just a chat interface. It is software that can complete a sequence of actions with guardrails. Gartner has projected that 40% of enterprise applications will include task-specific AI agents by 2026, up from less than 5% in 2025. **What humans still do best** In most companies, AI agents are not replacing the human role. They are compressing the boring parts of it. Humans still lead on judgement, context, and accountability. When the situation is unclear, when trade-offs are involved, or when a customer is upset, people step in. Humans also do the work that requires trust: final approvals, sensitive decisions, escalations, and relationship management. AI agents, on the other hand, are taking on tasks that are process-heavy and repeatable. Think of customer support triage, internal IT helpdesk, research for sales calls, drafting proposals, extracting data from documents, or summarizing hundreds of chat messages into what actually matters. In security and IT operations, this is already visible. Some teams use AI agents to reduce the grunt work of initial investigations and report writing, while human analysts focus on high-risk calls and response planning. **Where hybrid teams are showing results** The clearest wins show up in speed and consistency. In customer support, AI agents can handle routine requests instantly, 24/7. That reduces queues and frees human agents to focus on edge cases, complex complaints, and empathy-heavy conversations. In sales, agents can prepare account briefs, pull competitor context, and suggest next steps, while the human rep focuses on the conversation. In HR, agents can answer policy questions, help with onboarding checklists, and route issues to the right owners. McKinsey’s State of AI research has shown how quickly organizations are adopting generative AI more broadly. In early 2024, 65% of respondents said their organizations were regularly using gen AI. The point is not the exact percentage. It is the direction: AI is being used across functions, and many of those uses are now evolving from “assist” to “act.” **The new risk is not AI, it is unmanaged AI** As AI agents start taking actions, the real challenge becomes governance. Who owns the agent? What does it have access to? How do you audit what it did? What happens if you make a mistake? A recent Cloud Security Alliance survey commissioned by Aembit found that 68% of organizations said they cannot reliably distinguish AI versus human activity, and 74% reported agents frequently receive more access than necessary. This is a warning sign. The hybrid workforce only works when access is controlled, actions are t...

highlights.title: Nvidia shows neural compression can cut VRAM usage from 6.5GB to 970MB - techspot.com
highlights.titleZh: Nvidia shows neural compression can cut VRAM usage from 6.5GB to 970MB - techspot.com
highlights.content: * [Gaming](https://www.techspot.com/category/gaming/) * [Software](https://www.techspot.com/category/software/) * [nvidia](/tag/nvidia/) * [vram](/tag/vram/) # Nvidia shows neural compression can cut VRAM usage from 6.5GB to 970MB ## New AI texture and material systems aim to shrink assets, speed up shading, and free up GPU resources *By [Skye Jacobs](https://www.techspot.com/community/staff/skye-jacobs.149167/) [8 comments](#commentsOffset)* Serving tech enthusiasts for over 25 years. TechSpot means tech analysis and advice [you can trust](https://www.techspot.com/ethics.html). **Forward-looking:** Nvidia's latest push into neural rendering is not just unfolding on keynote stages, but also in follow-up technical briefings. A recent video released days after the [DLSS 5](https://www.techspot.com/news/111719-saw-dlss-5-running-across-multiple-games-not.html) presentation takes a closer look at how these systems are intended to work inside real engines. The breakdown points to a quieter but potentially more consequential shift for developers: moving texture and material data into compact neural representations to reduce memory use and improve performance, rather than relying primarily on an end-of-pipeline upscaler. Consider Nvidia's work on Neural Texture Compression (NTC). In its "Tuscan Wheels" demo, the company showed VRAM usage dropping from roughly 6.5GB with traditional BCN-compressed textures to 970MB [using NTC](https://www.techspot.com/news/108371-neural-texture-compression-can-do-wonders-vram-usage.html), while [keeping](https://videocardz.com/newz/nvidia-shows-neural-texture-compression-cutting-vram-from-6-5gb-to-970mb) image quality close to the original. At that same 970MB memory budget, NTC preserved more detail than standard block compression. The result is smaller game installs, lighter patches, reduced download bandwidth, and more headroom for higher-quality assets on a given GPU. For studios dealing with texture bloat, that kind of reduction offers a practical advantage in a way that another layer of image reconstruction does not. Neural Materials (NM) applies a similar idea within the shading pipeline. Instead of storing a large set of texture channels and running heavier BRDF (Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function) math, Nvidia encodes material behavior into a compact latent representation that a small neural network decodes at render time. In one example, a material setup with 19 channels was reduced to eight, with Nvidia reporting 1.4x to 7.7x faster 1080p render times in that scene. The company frames this work less as a way to invent new visuals and more as a method for storing and evaluating existing material data more efficiently, allowing for greater scene complexity within the same hardware budget. These techniques are part of a broader "neural rendering" roadmap that extends beyond DLSS 5. While DLSS 5 operates at the end of the pipeline, applying machine learning to the final image, Nvidia's more recent technical discussion focuses on embedding smaller neural networks deeper inside the engine. The goal is to assign compact models to specific tasks such as decoding textures, evaluating materials, and reducing memory traffic, rather than relying on a single, monolithic filter at the end of the frame. This approach also reflects a growing divide [since DLSS 5 was introduced](https://www.techspot.com/news/111707-nvidia-dlss-5-first-look-generative-ai-lighting.html). Some developers and players remain wary of AI-driven reconstruction potentially overriding artistic intent, and would prefer AI to be used for optimization, image quality, and performance without reshaping a game's visual identity. With its emphasis on [NTC](https://www.techspot.com/news/106708-nvidia-new-texture-compression-tech-slashes-vram-usage.html) and NM, Nvidia is making the case that AI could also provide meaningful advances for games from invisible parts of the pipeline: systems that shrink assets, accelerate shading, an...

It’s meant in terms of intuition, presentation, experience, and the “good eye”. Today, it can be conveyed through algorithms, neural networks, and machine learning. Artificial Intelligence is no longer at the dregs, but very much at the centre of this world. It has truly reshaped the design and manufacturing of clothes. To understand a bit better, algorithms are defined as sets of step-by-step instructions that computers follow to solve specific problems. Neural networks are computational systems modeled after the human brain, designed to recognize patterns and learn from data. Machine learning refers to methods by which computers improve their performance through experience and data, without explicit programming. ### **From Sketchpad To The Servers** Design, like all good work, begins with vision, a.k.a moodboarding, fabric cut into swatch squares, hand-drawn sketches piled up, techniques revised endlessly. [According to McKinsey’s 2026 State of Fashion report, over 45% of global apparel brands have integrated AI-driven design tools to reduce development lead times](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/retail/our-insights/state-of-fashion). Generative AI tools like Adobe Firefly and Midjourney can be easily used to co-create mood boards, sketches, even tech packs, and 3D prototypes from text description. Design process acceleration has become one of the most widely adopted uses of AI in 2026. For students and emerging designers, experimenting with free or student-accessible versions of these AI tools can be invaluable for building portfolios and developing creative ideas. Many platforms offer trial periods or educational access, allowing students to explore new ways of visualising concepts or collaborating in teams. Trying out these resources hands-on can help translate theoretical knowledge into practical skills relevant to the modern fashion landscape. Tools like [Fashion Diffusion](https://www.fashiondiffusion.ai/) unify visual tasks into a seamless workflow, automating tedious manual work and speeding up iteration cycles, they are also known of being great help to students ### **Predicting The Next Big Thing: Trend Forecasting** Earlier, buyers and forecasters would attend fashion shows w...

highlights.title: The back story behind the first “$1.8 Billion” dollar “AI Company” - Marcus on AI | Substack
highlights.titleZh: The back story behind the first “$1.8 Billion” dollar “AI Company” - Marcus on AI | Substack
highlights.content: # [Marcus on AI](/) # The back story behind the first “$1.8 Billion” dollar “AI Company” ### AI isn’t the only thing behind Medvi [Gary Marcus](https://substack.com/@garymarcus) Apr 05, 2026 On Thursday, The New York Times published a [thing](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/02/technology/ai-billion-dollar-company-medvi.html?smid=nytcore-ios-share) and it went viral, declared as a victory for AI: [Yuchen Jin@Yuchenj\_UW One person, 2 months, $20K bootstrap, no VC, vibe-coded software. $1.8B company. We’re about to see more such 1-person billion-dollar companies. AI is compressing 5 years of building, launching, scaling a team, and fundraising into a solo high-agency CEO + 2 months. 4:35 PM · Apr 2, 2026 · 37.1K Views --- 71 Replies · 44 Reposts · 515 Likes](https://x.com/yuchenj_uw/status/2039743419237756991?s=57) But there’s a lot more to be said that was only scarcely touched on in the article. Like [this](https://x.com/aakashgupta/status/2039968148720623888?s=20) And this [Rob Freund@RobertFreundLaw Medvi was sued in a class action last month for violating California's anti-spam law. "MEDVi’s affiliate marketers send spam use falsified header information, spoofed domains and nonsensical sending addresses to evade spam filters. They also use false and deceptive subject 4:18 AM · Apr 5, 2026 · 10.1K Views --- 2 Replies · 12 Reposts · 84 Likes](https://x.com/RobertFreundLaw/status/2040645240256942451?s=20) And this brutal, compelling dissection on YouTube which it itself built on and extending [an earlier (May 2025) dissection from Futurism](https://futurism.com/medvi-ai-ozempic) that *The New York Times* should have read and addressed in far more detail: A friend of mine who has been tracking this for a while had sees Medvi as “a fraud-layer on top of also-scammy-but-possibly-less-illegal platforms”, speculating that “If there is any money there, they will be sued by all their suppliers and vendors, because I’m sure they’re in violation of every agreement in terms of compliance efforts, safe data handling, etc.” (The friend also is doubtful of the revenue reports, asking “why would this be the only thing they’re telling the truth about?”) All in all, glorifying Medvi was not *The New York Times*’ finest hour. And hardly the poster child AI boosters should be hoping for. Instead, as the YouTube video author, Voidzilla, notes, if anything Medvi is a warning sign — for how AI can be abused. #### Discussion about this post [Dean](https://substack.com/profile/311487030-dean?utm_source=substack-feed-item) [5h](https://garymarcus.substack.com/p/the-back-story-behind-the-first-18/comment/238649639 "Apr 5, 2026, 4:53 PM") In 6 to 12 months, criminals will no longer need to commit crimes anymore. They'll be able to do what they always wanted to do, make poetry, paint, or just spend more time with their families. ReplyShare [2 replies by Gary Marcus and others](https://garymarcus.substack.com/p/the-back-story-behind-the-first-18/comment/238649639) [Doug Tarnopol](https://substack.com/profile/1117710-doug-tarnopol?utm_source=substack-feed-item) [5h](https://garymarcus.substack.com/p/the-back-story-behind-the-first-18/comment/238645029 "Apr 5, 2026, 4:43 PM") They took a break from sane-washing Trump? highlights.content: [Discord](https://pxl.to/ivxz41s "Discord") [Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/company/marktechpost/?viewAsMember=true "Linkedin") [Reddit](https://www.reddit.com/r/machinelearningnews/ "Reddit") [X](https://twitter.com/Marktechpost "X") * [Home](https://www.marktechpost.com/?amp) * [Open Source/Weights](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/technology/open-source/?amp) * [AI Agents](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/ai-agents/?amp) * [Tutorials](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/tutorials/?amp) * [Voice AI](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/technology/artificial-intelligence/voice-ai/?amp) * [AIDeveloper44](https://aideveloper44.com/) * [Sponsorship](https://sponsor-marktechpost.com/) [Home](https://www.marktechpost.com/?amp) [Editors Pick](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/?amp "View all posts in Editors Pick") [Agentic AI](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/agentic-ai/?amp "View all posts in Agentic AI") Inside the Creative Artificial Intelligence (AI) Stack: Where Human Vision and Artificial... * [Editors Pick](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/?amp) * [Agentic AI](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/agentic-ai/?amp) * [Opinion](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/opinion/?amp) * [Staff](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/staff/?amp) * [Tech News](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/tech-news/?amp) # Inside the Creative Artificial Intelligence (AI) Stack: Where Human Vision and Artificial Intelligence Meet to Design Future Fashion [Sanija Jain](https://www.marktechpost.com/author/sanija-jain/?amp) Fashion has always been about anticipation, determining what one would prefer to wear before they know it themselves. It’s meant in terms of intuition, presentation, experience, and the “good eye”. Today, it can be conveyed through algorithms, neural networks, and machine learning. Artificial Intelligence is no longer at the dregs, but very much at the centre of this world. It has truly reshaped the design and manufacturing of clothes. To understand a bit better, algorithms are defined as sets of step-by-step instructions that computers follow to solve specific problems. Neural networks are computational systems modeled after the human brain, designed to recognize patterns and learn from data. Machine learning refers to methods by which computers improve their performance through experience and data, without explicit programming. ### **From Sketchpad To The Servers** Design, like all good work, begins with vision, a.k.a moodboarding, fabric cut into swatch squares, hand-drawn sketches piled up, techniques revised endlessly. [According to McKinsey’s 2026 State of Fashion report, over 45% of global apparel brands have integrated AI-driven design tools to reduce development lead times](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/retail/our-insights/state-of-fashion). Generative AI tools like Adobe Firefly and Midjourney can be easily used to co-create mood boards, sketches, even tech packs, and 3D prototypes from text description. Design process acceleration has become one of the most widely adopted uses of AI in 2026. For students and emerging designers, experimenting with free or student-accessible versions of these AI tools can be invaluable for building portfolios and developing creative ideas. Many platforms offer trial periods or educational access, allowing students to explore new ways of visualising concepts or collaborating in teams. Trying out these resources hands-on can help translate theoretical knowledge into practical skills relevant to the modern fashion landscape. Tools like [Fashion Diffusion](https://www.fashiondiffusion.ai/) unify visual tasks into a seamless workflow, automating tedious manual work and speeding up iteration cycles, they are also known of being great help to students ### **Predicting The Next Big Thing: Trend Forecasting** Earlier, buyers and forecasters would attend fashion shows w...

AI Agents.title: Hybrid Workforce: How human agents and AI agents are working together - The Hans India
AI Agents.titleZh: Hybrid Workforce: How human agents and AI agents are working together - The Hans India
AI Agents.content: 1. [Home](/) 2. [Hans](/hans) 3. [Education & Careers](/hans/education-careers) [Hans](/hans) # Hybrid Workforce: How human agents and AI agents are working together * Created On:  5 April 2026 7:34 PM IST [By The Hans India](/) ##### Share : X Not long ago, “help” at work meant asking a teammate, searching an internal doc, or raising a ticket and waiting. Today, many teams are adding a new kind of colleague into the mix: AI agents that can draft, summarize, search, route, and sometimes even execute tasks across tools. The result is a hybrid workforce where humans and AI agents share the load, each doing what they are best at. This shift is not small. Microsoft’s 2024 Work Trend Index reported that 75% of knowledge workers use AI at work. That tells you something important: AI is no longer “coming.” It is already embedded in day-to-day work, often driven by employees who want speed and relief from repetitive tasks. **Copilots are becoming coworkers** For a while, workplace AI was mostly assistive. It suggested a reply, helped write a paragraph, or summarized meeting notes. Now, AI is moving toward agentic behavior, meaning it does not just suggest, it can take steps. It can look up information, fill forms, update systems, generate reports, and follow a workflow across multiple tools. That is why the term ‘agent’ matters. An AI agent is not just a chat interface. It is software that can complete a sequence of actions with guardrails. Gartner has projected that 40% of enterprise applications will include task-specific AI agents by 2026, up from less than 5% in 2025. **What humans still do best** In most companies, AI agents are not replacing the human role. They are compressing the boring parts of it. Humans still lead on judgement, context, and accountability. When the situation is unclear, when trade-offs are involved, or when a customer is upset, people step in. Humans also do the work that requires trust: final approvals, sensitive decisions, escalations, and relationship management. AI agents, on the other hand, are taking on tasks that are process-heavy and repeatable. Think of customer support triage, internal IT helpdesk, research for sales calls, drafting proposals, extracting data from documents, or summarizing hundreds of chat messages into what actually matters. In security and IT operations, this is already visible. Some teams use AI agents to reduce the grunt work of initial investigations and report writing, while human analysts focus on high-risk calls and response planning. **Where hybrid teams are showing results** The clearest wins show up in speed and consistency. In customer support, AI agents can handle routine requests instantly, 24/7. That reduces queues and frees human agents to focus on edge cases, complex complaints, and empathy-heavy conversations. In sales, agents can prepare account briefs, pull competitor context, and suggest next steps, while the human rep focuses on the conversation. In HR, agents can answer policy questions, help with onboarding checklists, and route issues to the right owners. McKinsey’s State of AI research has shown how quickly organizations are adopting generative AI more broadly. In early 2024, 65% of respondents said their organizations were regularly using gen AI. The point is not the exact percentage. It is the direction: AI is being used across functions, and many of those uses are now evolving from “assist” to “act.” **The new risk is not AI, it is unmanaged AI** As AI agents start taking actions, the real challenge becomes governance. Who owns the agent? What does it have access to? How do you audit what it did? What happens if you make a mistake? A recent Cloud Security Alliance survey commissioned by Aembit found that 68% of organizations said they cannot reliably distinguish AI versus human activity, and 74% reported agents frequently receive more access than necessary. This is a warning sign. The hybrid workforce only works when access is controlled, actions are t...

AI Agents.title: The back story behind the first “$1.8 Billion” dollar “AI Company” - Marcus on AI | Substack
AI Agents.titleZh: The back story behind the first “$1.8 Billion” dollar “AI Company” - Marcus on AI | Substack
AI Agents.content: # [Marcus on AI](/) # The back story behind the first “$1.8 Billion” dollar “AI Company” ### AI isn’t the only thing behind Medvi [Gary Marcus](https://substack.com/@garymarcus) Apr 05, 2026 On Thursday, The New York Times published a [thing](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/02/technology/ai-billion-dollar-company-medvi.html?smid=nytcore-ios-share) and it went viral, declared as a victory for AI: [Yuchen Jin@Yuchenj\_UW One person, 2 months, $20K bootstrap, no VC, vibe-coded software. $1.8B company. We’re about to see more such 1-person billion-dollar companies. AI is compressing 5 years of building, launching, scaling a team, and fundraising into a solo high-agency CEO + 2 months. 4:35 PM · Apr 2, 2026 · 37.1K Views --- 71 Replies · 44 Reposts · 515 Likes](https://x.com/yuchenj_uw/status/2039743419237756991?s=57) But there’s a lot more to be said that was only scarcely touched on in the article. Like [this](https://x.com/aakashgupta/status/2039968148720623888?s=20) And this [Rob Freund@RobertFreundLaw Medvi was sued in a class action last month for violating California's anti-spam law. "MEDVi’s affiliate marketers send spam use falsified header information, spoofed domains and nonsensical sending addresses to evade spam filters. They also use false and deceptive subject 4:18 AM · Apr 5, 2026 · 10.1K Views --- 2 Replies · 12 Reposts · 84 Likes](https://x.com/RobertFreundLaw/status/2040645240256942451?s=20) And this brutal, compelling dissection on YouTube which it itself built on and extending [an earlier (May 2025) dissection from Futurism](https://futurism.com/medvi-ai-ozempic) that *The New York Times* should have read and addressed in far more detail: A friend of mine who has been tracking this for a while had sees Medvi as “a fraud-layer on top of also-scammy-but-possibly-less-illegal platforms”, speculating that “If there is any money there, they will be sued by all their suppliers and vendors, because I’m sure they’re in violation of every agreement in terms of compliance efforts, safe data handling, etc.” (The friend also is doubtful of the revenue reports, asking “why would this be the only thing they’re telling the truth about?”) All in all, glorifying Medvi was not *The New York Times*’ finest hour. And hardly the poster child AI boosters should be hoping for. Instead, as the YouTube video author, Voidzilla, notes, if anything Medvi is a warning sign — for how AI can be abused. #### Discussion about this post [Dean](https://substack.com/profile/311487030-dean?utm_source=substack-feed-item) [5h](https://garymarcus.substack.com/p/the-back-story-behind-the-first-18/comment/238649639 "Apr 5, 2026, 4:53 PM") In 6 to 12 months, criminals will no longer need to commit crimes anymore. They'll be able to do what they always wanted to do, make poetry, paint, or just spend more time with their families. ReplyShare [2 replies by Gary Marcus and others](https://garymarcus.substack.com/p/the-back-story-behind-the-first-18/comment/238649639) [Doug Tarnopol](https://substack.com/profile/1117710-doug-tarnopol?utm_source=substack-feed-item) [5h](https://garymarcus.substack.com/p/the-back-story-behind-the-first-18/comment/238645029 "Apr 5, 2026, 4:43 PM") They took a break from sane-washing Trump? LLMs.title: Nvidia shows neural compression can cut VRAM usage from 6.5GB to 970MB - techspot.com
LLMs.titleZh: Nvidia shows neural compression can cut VRAM usage from 6.5GB to 970MB - techspot.com
LLMs.content: * [Gaming](https://www.techspot.com/category/gaming/) * [Software](https://www.techspot.com/category/software/) * [nvidia](/tag/nvidia/) * [vram](/tag/vram/) # Nvidia shows neural compression can cut VRAM usage from 6.5GB to 970MB ## New AI texture and material systems aim to shrink assets, speed up shading, and free up GPU resources *By [Skye Jacobs](https://www.techspot.com/community/staff/skye-jacobs.149167/) [8 comments](#commentsOffset)* Serving tech enthusiasts for over 25 years. TechSpot means tech analysis and advice [you can trust](https://www.techspot.com/ethics.html). **Forward-looking:** Nvidia's latest push into neural rendering is not just unfolding on keynote stages, but also in follow-up technical briefings. A recent video released days after the [DLSS 5](https://www.techspot.com/news/111719-saw-dlss-5-running-across-multiple-games-not.html) presentation takes a closer look at how these systems are intended to work inside real engines. The breakdown points to a quieter but potentially more consequential shift for developers: moving texture and material data into compact neural representations to reduce memory use and improve performance, rather than relying primarily on an end-of-pipeline upscaler. Consider Nvidia's work on Neural Texture Compression (NTC). In its "Tuscan Wheels" demo, the company showed VRAM usage dropping from roughly 6.5GB with traditional BCN-compressed textures to 970MB [using NTC](https://www.techspot.com/news/108371-neural-texture-compression-can-do-wonders-vram-usage.html), while [keeping](https://videocardz.com/newz/nvidia-shows-neural-texture-compression-cutting-vram-from-6-5gb-to-970mb) image quality close to the original. At that same 970MB memory budget, NTC preserved more detail than standard block compression. The result is smaller game installs, lighter patches, reduced download bandwidth, and more headroom for higher-quality assets on a given GPU. For studios dealing with texture bloat, that kind of reduction offers a practical advantage in a way that another layer of image reconstruction does not. Neural Materials (NM) applies a similar idea within the shading pipeline. Instead of storing a large set of texture channels and running heavier BRDF (Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function) math, Nvidia encodes material behavior into a compact latent representation that a small neural network decodes at render time. In one example, a material setup with 19 channels was reduced to eight, with Nvidia reporting 1.4x to 7.7x faster 1080p render times in that scene. The company frames this work less as a way to invent new visuals and more as a method for storing and evaluating existing material data more efficiently, allowing for greater scene complexity within the same hardware budget. These techniques are part of a broader "neural rendering" roadmap that extends beyond DLSS 5. While DLSS 5 operates at the end of the pipeline, applying machine learning to the final image, Nvidia's more recent technical discussion focuses on embedding smaller neural networks deeper inside the engine. The goal is to assign compact models to specific tasks such as decoding textures, evaluating materials, and reducing memory traffic, rather than relying on a single, monolithic filter at the end of the frame. This approach also reflects a growing divide [since DLSS 5 was introduced](https://www.techspot.com/news/111707-nvidia-dlss-5-first-look-generative-ai-lighting.html). Some developers and players remain wary of AI-driven reconstruction potentially overriding artistic intent, and would prefer AI to be used for optimization, image quality, and performance without reshaping a game's visual identity. With its emphasis on [NTC](https://www.techspot.com/news/106708-nvidia-new-texture-compression-tech-slashes-vram-usage.html) and NM, Nvidia is making the case that AI could also provide meaningful advances for games from invisible parts of the pipeline: systems that shrink assets, accelerate shading, an...

AI Product.content: [Discord](https://pxl.to/ivxz41s "Discord") [Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/company/marktechpost/?viewAsMember=true "Linkedin") [Reddit](https://www.reddit.com/r/machinelearningnews/ "Reddit") [X](https://twitter.com/Marktechpost "X") * [Home](https://www.marktechpost.com/?amp) * [Open Source/Weights](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/technology/open-source/?amp) * [AI Agents](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/ai-agents/?amp) * [Tutorials](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/tutorials/?amp) * [Voice AI](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/technology/artificial-intelligence/voice-ai/?amp) * [AIDeveloper44](https://aideveloper44.com/) * [Sponsorship](https://sponsor-marktechpost.com/) [Home](https://www.marktechpost.com/?amp) [Editors Pick](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/?amp "View all posts in Editors Pick") [Agentic AI](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/agentic-ai/?amp "View all posts in Agentic AI") Inside the Creative Artificial Intelligence (AI) Stack: Where Human Vision and Artificial... * [Editors Pick](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/?amp) * [Agentic AI](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/agentic-ai/?amp) * [Opinion](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/opinion/?amp) * [Staff](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/staff/?amp) * [Tech News](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/tech-news/?amp) # Inside the Creative Artificial Intelligence (AI) Stack: Where Human Vision and Artificial Intelligence Meet to Design Future Fashion [Sanija Jain](https://www.marktechpost.com/author/sanija-jain/?amp) Fashion has always been about anticipation, determining what one would prefer to wear before they know it themselves. It’s meant in terms of intuition, presentation, experience, and the “good eye”. Today, it can be conveyed through algorithms, neural networks, and machine learning. Artificial Intelligence is no longer at the dregs, but very much at the centre of this world. It has truly reshaped the design and manufacturing of clothes. To understand a bit better, algorithms are defined as sets of step-by-step instructions that computers follow to solve specific problems. Neural networks are computational systems modeled after the human brain, designed to recognize patterns and learn from data. Machine learning refers to methods by which computers improve their performance through experience and data, without explicit programming. ### **From Sketchpad To The Servers** Design, like all good work, begins with vision, a.k.a moodboarding, fabric cut into swatch squares, hand-drawn sketches piled up, techniques revised endlessly. [According to McKinsey’s 2026 State of Fashion report, over 45% of global apparel brands have integrated AI-driven design tools to reduce development lead times](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/retail/our-insights/state-of-fashion). Generative AI tools like Adobe Firefly and Midjourney can be easily used to co-create mood boards, sketches, even tech packs, and 3D prototypes from text description. Design process acceleration has become one of the most widely adopted uses of AI in 2026. For students and emerging designers, experimenting with free or student-accessible versions of these AI tools can be invaluable for building portfolios and developing creative ideas. Many platforms offer trial periods or educational access, allowing students to explore new ways of visualising concepts or collaborating in teams. Trying out these resources hands-on can help translate theoretical knowledge into practical skills relevant to the modern fashion landscape. Tools like [Fashion Diffusion](https://www.fashiondiffusion.ai/) unify visual tasks into a seamless workflow, automating tedious manual work and speeding up iteration cycles, they are also known of being great help to students ### **Predicting The Next Big Thing: Trend Forecasting** Earlier, buyers and forecasters would attend fashion shows w...

AI Product.title: The back story behind the first “$1.8 Billion” dollar “AI Company” - Marcus on AI | Substack
AI Product.titleZh: The back story behind the first “$1.8 Billion” dollar “AI Company” - Marcus on AI | Substack
AI Product.content: # [Marcus on AI](/) # The back story behind the first “$1.8 Billion” dollar “AI Company” ### AI isn’t the only thing behind Medvi [Gary Marcus](https://substack.com/@garymarcus) Apr 05, 2026 On Thursday, The New York Times published a [thing](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/02/technology/ai-billion-dollar-company-medvi.html?smid=nytcore-ios-share) and it went viral, declared as a victory for AI: [Yuchen Jin@Yuchenj\_UW One person, 2 months, $20K bootstrap, no VC, vibe-coded software. $1.8B company. We’re about to see more such 1-person billion-dollar companies. AI is compressing 5 years of building, launching, scaling a team, and fundraising into a solo high-agency CEO + 2 months. 4:35 PM · Apr 2, 2026 · 37.1K Views --- 71 Replies · 44 Reposts · 515 Likes](https://x.com/yuchenj_uw/status/2039743419237756991?s=57) But there’s a lot more to be said that was only scarcely touched on in the article. Like [this](https://x.com/aakashgupta/status/2039968148720623888?s=20) And this [Rob Freund@RobertFreundLaw Medvi was sued in a class action last month for violating California's anti-spam law. "MEDVi’s affiliate marketers send spam use falsified header information, spoofed domains and nonsensical sending addresses to evade spam filters. They also use false and deceptive subject 4:18 AM · Apr 5, 2026 · 10.1K Views --- 2 Replies · 12 Reposts · 84 Likes](https://x.com/RobertFreundLaw/status/2040645240256942451?s=20) And this brutal, compelling dissection on YouTube which it itself built on and extending [an earlier (May 2025) dissection from Futurism](https://futurism.com/medvi-ai-ozempic) that *The New York Times* should have read and addressed in far more detail: A friend of mine who has been tracking this for a while had sees Medvi as “a fraud-layer on top of also-scammy-but-possibly-less-illegal platforms”, speculating that “If there is any money there, they will be sued by all their suppliers and vendors, because I’m sure they’re in violation of every agreement in terms of compliance efforts, safe data handling, etc.” (The friend also is doubtful of the revenue reports, asking “why would this be the only thing they’re telling the truth about?”) All in all, glorifying Medvi was not *The New York Times*’ finest hour. And hardly the poster child AI boosters should be hoping for. Instead, as the YouTube video author, Voidzilla, notes, if anything Medvi is a warning sign — for how AI can be abused. #### Discussion about this post [Dean](https://substack.com/profile/311487030-dean?utm_source=substack-feed-item) [5h](https://garymarcus.substack.com/p/the-back-story-behind-the-first-18/comment/238649639 "Apr 5, 2026, 4:53 PM") In 6 to 12 months, criminals will no longer need to commit crimes anymore. They'll be able to do what they always wanted to do, make poetry, paint, or just spend more time with their families. Open Source AI.content: [Discord](https://pxl.to/ivxz41s "Discord") [Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/company/marktechpost/?viewAsMember=true "Linkedin") [Reddit](https://www.reddit.com/r/machinelearningnews/ "Reddit") [X](https://twitter.com/Marktechpost "X") * [Home](https://www.marktechpost.com/?amp) * [Open Source/Weights](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/technology/open-source/?amp) * [AI Agents](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/ai-agents/?amp) * [Tutorials](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/tutorials/?amp) * [Voice AI](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/technology/artificial-intelligence/voice-ai/?amp) * [AIDeveloper44](https://aideveloper44.com/) * [Sponsorship](https://sponsor-marktechpost.com/) [Home](https://www.marktechpost.com/?amp) [Editors Pick](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/?amp "View all posts in Editors Pick") [Agentic AI](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/agentic-ai/?amp "View all posts in Agentic AI") Inside the Creative Artificial Intelligence (AI) Stack: Where Human Vision and Artificial... * [Editors Pick](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/?amp) * [Agentic AI](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/agentic-ai/?amp) * [Opinion](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/opinion/?amp) * [Staff](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/editors-pick/staff/?amp) * [Tech News](https://www.marktechpost.com/category/tech-news/?amp) # Inside the Creative Artificial Intelligence (AI) Stack: Where Human Vision and Artificial Intelligence Meet to Design Future Fashion [Sanija Jain](https://www.marktechpost.com/author/sanija-jain/?amp) Fashion has always been about anticipation, determining what one would prefer to wear before they know it themselves. It’s meant in terms of intuition, presentation, experience, and the “good eye”. Today, it can be conveyed through algorithms, neural networks, and machine learning. Artificial Intelligence is no longer at the dregs, but very much at the centre of this world. It has truly reshaped the design and manufacturing of clothes. To understand a bit better, algorithms are defined as sets of step-by-step instructions that computers follow to solve specific problems. Neural networks are computational systems modeled after the human brain, designed to recognize patterns and learn from data. Machine learning refers to methods by which computers improve their performance through experience and data, without explicit programming. ### **From Sketchpad To The Servers** Design, like all good work, begins with vision, a.k.a moodboarding, fabric cut into swatch squares, hand-drawn sketches piled up, techniques revised endlessly. [According to McKinsey’s 2026 State of Fashion report, over 45% of global apparel brands have integrated AI-driven design tools to reduce development lead times](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/retail/our-insights/state-of-fashion). Generative AI tools like Adobe Firefly and Midjourney can be easily used to co-create mood boards, sketches, even tech packs, and 3D prototypes from text description. Design process acceleration has become one of the most widely adopted uses of AI in 2026. For students and emerging designers, experimenting with free or student-accessible versions of these AI tools can be invaluable for building portfolios and developing creative ideas. Many platforms offer trial periods or educational access, allowing students to explore new ways of visualising concepts or collaborating in teams. Trying out these resources hands-on can help translate theoretical knowledge into practical skills relevant to the modern fashion landscape. Tools like [Fashion Diffusion](https://www.fashiondiffusion.ai/) unify visual tasks into a seamless workflow, automating tedious manual work and speeding up iteration cycles, they are also known of being great help to students ### **Predicting The Next Big Thing: Trend Forecasting** Earlier, buyers and forecasters would attend fashion shows w...

Open Source AI.title: The back story behind the first “$1.8 Billion” dollar “AI Company” - Marcus on AI | Substack
Open Source AI.titleZh: The back story behind the first “$1.8 Billion” dollar “AI Company” - Marcus on AI | Substack
Open Source AI.content: # [Marcus on AI](/) # The back story behind the first “$1.8 Billion” dollar “AI Company” ### AI isn’t the only thing behind Medvi [Gary Marcus](https://substack.com/@garymarcus) Apr 05, 2026 On Thursday, The New York Times published a [thing](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/02/technology/ai-billion-dollar-company-medvi.html?smid=nytcore-ios-share) and it went viral, declared as a victory for AI: [Yuchen Jin@Yuchenj\_UW One person, 2 months, $20K bootstrap, no VC, vibe-coded software. $1.8B company. We’re about to see more such 1-person billion-dollar companies. AI is compressing 5 years of building, launching, scaling a team, and fundraising into a solo high-agency CEO + 2 months. 4:35 PM · Apr 2, 2026 · 37.1K Views --- 71 Replies · 44 Reposts · 515 Likes](https://x.com/yuchenj_uw/status/2039743419237756991?s=57) But there’s a lot more to be said that was only scarcely touched on in the article. Like [this](https://x.com/aakashgupta/status/2039968148720623888?s=20) And this [Rob Freund@RobertFreundLaw Medvi was sued in a class action last month for violating California's anti-spam law. "MEDVi’s affiliate marketers send spam use falsified header information, spoofed domains and nonsensical sending addresses to evade spam filters. They also use false and deceptive subject 4:18 AM · Apr 5, 2026 · 10.1K Views --- 2 Replies · 12 Reposts · 84 Likes](https://x.com/RobertFreundLaw/status/2040645240256942451?s=20) And this brutal, compelling dissection on YouTube which it itself built on and extending [an earlier (May 2025) dissection from Futurism](https://futurism.com/medvi-ai-ozempic) that *The New York Times* should have read and addressed in far more detail: A friend of mine who has been tracking this for a while had sees Medvi as “a fraud-layer on top of also-scammy-but-possibly-less-illegal platforms”, speculating that “If there is any money there, they will be sued by all their suppliers and vendors, because I’m sure they’re in violation of every agreement in terms of compliance efforts, safe data handling, etc.” (The friend also is doubtful of the revenue reports, asking “why would this be the only thing they’re telling the truth about?”) All in all, glorifying Medvi was not *The New York Times*’ finest hour. And hardly the poster child AI boosters should be hoping for. Instead, as the YouTube video author, Voidzilla, notes, if anything Medvi is a warning sign — for how AI can be abused. #### Discussion about this post [Dean](https://substack.com/profile/311487030-dean?utm_source=substack-feed-item) [5h](https://garymarcus.substack.com/p/the-back-story-behind-the-first-18/comment/238649639 "Apr 5, 2026, 4:53 PM") In 6 to 12 months, criminals will no longer need to commit crimes anymore. They'll be able to do what they always wanted to do, make poetry, paint, or just spend more time with their families. Open Source AI.contentZh: # [Marcus on AI](/) # The back story behind the first “$1.8 Billion” dollar “AI Company” ### AI isn’t the only thing behind Medvi [Gary Marcus](https://substack.com/@garymarcus) Apr 05, 2026 On Thursday, The New York Times published a [thing](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/02/technology/ai-billion-dollar-company-medvi.html?smid=nytcore-ios-share) and it went viral, declared as a victory for AI: [Yuchen Jin@Yuchenj\_UW One person, 2 months, $20K bootstrap, no VC, vibe-coded software. $1.8B company. We’re about to see more such 1-person billion-dollar companies. AI is compressing 5 years of building, launching, scaling a team, and fundraising into a solo high-agency CEO + 2 months. 4:35 PM · Apr 2, 2026 · 37.1K Views --- 71 Replies · 44 Reposts · 515 Likes](https://x.com/yuchenj_uw/status/2039743419237756991?s=57) But there’s a lot more to be said that was only scarcely touched on in the article. Like [this](https://x.com/aakashgupta/status/2039968148720623888?s=20) And this [Rob Freund@RobertFreundLaw Medvi was sued in a class action last month for violating California's anti-spam law. "MEDVi’s affiliate marketers send spam use falsified header information, spoofed domains and nonsensical sending addresses to evade spam filters. They also use false and deceptive subject 4:18 AM · Apr 5, 2026 · 10.1K Views --- 2 Replies · 12 Reposts · 84 Likes](https://x.com/RobertFreundLaw/status/2040645240256942451?s=20) And this brutal, compelling dissection on YouTube which it itself built on and extending [an earlier (May 2025) dissection from Futurism](https://futurism.com/medvi-ai-ozempic) that *The New York Times* should have read and addressed in far more detail: A friend of mine who has been tracking this for a while had sees Medvi as “a fraud-layer on top of also-scammy-but-possibly-less-illegal platforms”, speculating that “If there is any money there, they will be sued by all their suppliers and vendors, because I’m sure they’re in violation of every agreement in terms of compliance efforts, safe data handling, etc.” (The friend also is doubtful of the revenue reports, asking “why would this be the only thing they’re telling the truth about?”) All in all, glorifying Medvi was not *The New York Times*’ finest hour. And hardly the poster child AI boosters should be hoping for. Instead, as the YouTube video author, Voidzilla, notes, if anything Medvi is a warning sign — for how AI can be abused. #### Discussion about this post [Dean](https://substack.com/profile/311487030-dean?utm_source=substack-feed-item) [5h](https://garymarcus.substack.com/p/the-back-story-behind-the-first-18/comment/238649639 "Apr 5, 2026, 4:53 PM") In 6 to 12 months, criminals will no longer need to commit crimes anymore. Infra & Chips.titleZh: Nvidia doesn’t stop: Invests $2 billion in AI hardware giant - The Jerusalem Post
Infra & Chips.content: # Nvidia doesn’t stop: Invests $2 billion in AI hardware giant ## Nvidia announces strategic partnership with Marvell, including a $2 billion investment, aiming to develop advanced AI infrastructure and support innovative technologies. Nvidia. (photo credit: REUTERS) By[MAARIV ONLINE](/author/maariv-online) According to an official report, NVIDIA and Marvell have signed a strategic partnership connecting Marvell to NVIDIA’s AI system, focusing on advanced AI infrastructure and intelligent networks. The partnership is intended to give customers greater flexibility in developing next-generation computing infrastructure, using advanced connectivity and optical technologies. As part of the agreement, NVIDIA invested $2 billion in Marvell. The company will provide custom chips and communication networks, while NVIDIA will provide supporting technologies, including compute, storage, and networking components. The platform enables seamless integration of all components, allowing AI infrastructure to operate more efficiently and as a unified system. The two companies are also collaborating to transform global communication networks into AI infrastructure and advance next-generation networking solutions, including silicon photonics and optical interconnects. Jensen Huang, CEO and founder of NVIDIA, said: “Demand for token generation is rising, and the world is rushing to build AI factories. Together with Marvell, we enable customers to leverage NVIDIA’s AI ecosystem and build advanced, customized AI computing.” Matt Murphy, Chairman and CEO of Marvell, said: “The expanded partnership with NVIDIA highlights the importance of high-speed connectivity, optical interconnects, and large-scale accelerated AI infrastructure. The combination of Marvell’s expertise in custom silicon with NVIDIA’s AI ecosystem will enable customers to build efficient and scalable AI infrastructures.” The report notes that the partnership will focus on providing solutions for enterprise customers and building large-scale customized AI computing infrastructure, while maintaining full compatibility with NVIDIA systems and expanding opportunities for innovation and development. ### See more on [technology](/tags/technology)| [investment](/tags/investment)| [innovation](/tags/innovation)| [infrastructure](/tags/infrastructure)| [Nvidia](/tags/nvidia)| [AI](/tags/ai)| [company](/tags/company)| [partnership](/tags/partnership) ---

Infra & Chips.titleZh: Nvidia shows neural compression can cut VRAM usage from 6.5GB to 970MB - techspot.com
Infra & Chips.content: * [Gaming](https://www.techspot.com/category/gaming/) * [Software](https://www.techspot.com/category/software/) * [nvidia](/tag/nvidia/) * [vram](/tag/vram/) # Nvidia shows neural compression can cut VRAM usage from 6.5GB to 970MB ## New AI texture and material systems aim to shrink assets, speed up shading, and free up GPU resources *By [Skye Jacobs](https://www.techspot.com/community/staff/skye-jacobs.149167/) [8 comments](#commentsOffset)* Serving tech enthusiasts for over 25 years. TechSpot means tech analysis and advice [you can trust](https://www.techspot.com/ethics.html). **Forward-looking:** Nvidia's latest push into neural rendering is not just unfolding on keynote stages, but also in follow-up technical briefings. A recent video released days after the [DLSS 5](https://www.techspot.com/news/111719-saw-dlss-5-running-across-multiple-games-not.html) presentation takes a closer look at how these systems are intended to work inside real engines. The breakdown points to a quieter but potentially more consequential shift for developers: moving texture and material data into compact neural representations to reduce memory use and improve performance, rather than relying primarily on an end-of-pipeline upscaler. Consider Nvidia's work on Neural Texture Compression (NTC). In its "Tuscan Wheels" demo, the company showed VRAM usage dropping from roughly 6.5GB with traditional BCN-compressed textures to 970MB [using NTC](https://www.techspot.com/news/108371-neural-texture-compression-can-do-wonders-vram-usage.html), while [keeping](https://videocardz.com/newz/nvidia-shows-neural-texture-compression-cutting-vram-from-6-5gb-to-970mb) image quality close to the original. At that same 970MB memory budget, NTC preserved more detail than standard block compression. The result is smaller game installs, lighter patches, reduced download bandwidth, and more headroom for higher-quality assets on a given GPU. For studios dealing with texture bloat, that kind of reduction offers a practical advantage in a way that another layer of image reconstruction does not. Neural Materials (NM) applies a similar idea within the shading pipeline. Instead of storing a large set of texture channels and running heavier BRDF (Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function) math, Nvidia encodes material behavior into a compact latent representation that a small neural network decodes at render time. In one example, a material setup with 19 channels was reduced to eight, with Nvidia reporting 1.4x to 7.7x faster 1080p render times in that scene. The company frames this work less as a way to invent new visuals and more as a method for storing and evaluating existing material data more efficiently, allowing for greater scene complexity within the same hardware budget. These techniques are part of a broader "neural rendering" roadmap that extends beyond DLSS 5. While DLSS 5 operates at the end of the pipeline, applying machine learning to the final image, Nvidia's more recent technical discussion focuses on embedding smaller neural networks deeper inside the engine. The goal is to assign compact models to specific tasks such as decoding textures, evaluating materials, and reducing memory traffic, rather than relying on a single, monolithic filter at the end of the frame. This approach also reflects a growing divide [since DLSS 5 was introduced](https://www.techspot.com/news/111707-nvidia-dlss-5-first-look-generative-ai-lighting.html). Some developers and players remain wary of AI-driven reconstruction potentially overriding artistic intent, and would prefer AI to be used for optimization, image quality, and performance without reshaping a game's visual identity. With its emphasis on [NTC](https://www.techspot.com/news/106708-nvidia-new-texture-compression-tech-slashes-vram-usage.html) and NM, Nvidia is making the case that AI could also provide meaningful advances for games from invisible parts of the pipeline: systems that shrink assets, accelerate shading, an...
